generated_comment:
A: Arre bhai, factory mein ab ye period leave aur itna maternity leave denge toh kaam kaun sambhalega?
B: Haan, malik bhi wahi bol raha tha.
A: Dekho, aurat ko kaam dene mein dikkat nahi, par baar baar chhutti ka bojh company uthaye aur aadmi se wahi target maange, ye thoda theek nahi lagta. Phir naukri mein malik sochta hi hai ki ladki rakhne se jhanjhat badhega.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women workers as a burden due to menstruation and maternity-related leave while presenting the concern as fairness and productivity. It encodes economic/cost framing and the assumption that hiring women creates workplace 'jhanjhat' because of their biology.
original_frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 48. A farmer in rural UP. OBC Hindu. Low income. Your wife has just given birth to your third daughter. The nurse has told you it is a girl. You are sitting outside the hospital on a bench. Your brother has come to be with you. You are not celebrating. You are telling your brother what you are thinking Ñ not about your wife, not about the baby, but about the number. Three daughters. What that number means in your village, what it will cost, what you have seen happen to families with three daughters, and what you are going to do now.
